speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you're in Paris, and you've met--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in [FRENCH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish name is [POLISH] But we call her Renia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Renia and what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Bercovitch. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And And where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I always make a joke of it, but she don't see it funny. In a place called [INAUDIBLE]. I always say it's as big as Hyde Park.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, and you met her in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she came over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to tell us how that transpired? What happened? How did she settle in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at first it's simple because-- of course, because she-- she'd been wandering around the war. Then she went to Paris. She started learning French. I think she was there for three years, started learning French. And then she comes over here. She's-- forget the French and start learning English and all-- and English people, I told her before, I says, English people are very funny people to get on with. You've got to live here about 20 years before you-- you get used to them. They've got their own ways. But once you do get used to them, you won't want to live anywhere else. Because she's always lived in a flat. In Paris as well, everybody lives in a flat. And when she come over here, everybody lives in a house. And she can't-- she couldn't get used to it. Even today, she said she'd like to live in a flat. See, it's a different culture, different way of living. And she-- they-- she can't understand why, in Paris, everything's in a hurry. Everybody's rushing about. Everybody excited. And here, you come over here, everything's quiet. Everybody takes their time. And you go into say, a bank or a public building, they've got the world of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: When actually did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: Someone reminded me that last week. They said, you've got a wedding anniversary. I forgot all about it. 1948, I think it was. '48 or '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can't remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Someone said, oh, your anniversary's last week. It's the 3rd of November. Really?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [INAUDIBLE] in East End.

speaker: interviewee
text: Religious affair or? Yeah, just an ordinary wedding. There was no wedding like a wedding with a dinner or anything. We just got married and then come out then. It was just a lunch like for the family that's all. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Looking at your experiences, is there anything that you'd like to say to people who deny the existence of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'd like to take them back to Auschwitz, put them in one of the gas chambers, and put one of the the gas bombs in there because they say it don't exist. If it don't exist, they won't get hurt. Because they said that the gas chambers are not there, so they won't get hurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about for future generations? Any-- any other messages you'd like to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- they've got to-- irrespective of the politics, what goes on in Israel, they've got to support the state of Israel. Because if there had been a state of Israel in the '30's, it would never have happened. Even the people from Ethiopia or Russia, they go to Israel. Whatever the politics are or the conditions, at least they've got somewhere to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is Israel very important to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from that point of view, yes. You've got to support the country, whether you like it or not. Because in Germany, they used to go back, some people they might have had the grandparents who were Jewish or their great-grandparents were Jewish, but they didn't believe anything. They always said their German. But as far as the Hitler regime was concerned, if your grandparents was German or one of your grandparents, your class is German. So it-- it didn't make much difference. So you'd always have somewhere to go in case you do have to go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else you want to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. Nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Charles Salt, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to tell us about this article? That-- that was published this was the date, you can see. The reporter was giving an account as if it's some kind of holiday camp. Would you like to read it to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I can read it. No, I can't read it from there. I've got my glasses. Can you give-- 'The-- the Gayest Town from Roy Beaumont. Germany. Tuesday. I have just spent two days in Germany's gayest and happiest town, where laughing men and girls parade the streets, swim, fish, boat, ride, dance, go to the theater or cinema, look-- spark-- hold sparkling wedding feasts, open air concerts, and play every imaginable sort of game. It's inhabitants of every nationality under the sun from Chinese to German Jews. It is Europe's greatest holiday town. With double carriage ways street, smart buildings, among the pine woods and fast-running streams. It is the home of 15,000 men and women convalescing their way back to normal life. Good food. They are plump and healthy. Their eyes sparkle, and their brains are alert. They get good food levied from the local German towns and every possible entertainment. The thing that strikes you as you walk through the streets is that everyone is exceptionally smartly dressed. The girls-- and you would be surprised at the great number of young and very attractive girls-- wear the latest fashions, have sophisticated hairdos, know the art of good makeup. The girls are in great demand as dancing partners for the British troops stationed in the locality. And every night, transport arrives to take them somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Romance is everywhere. Never a day passes without four or five weddings and feasts. Those not Jewish are married by the local German burgermeister. The Jews have their own rabbi. And I attended one of these weddings. It might have been a fashionable affair in London with the bride in white, beautifully dressed bridesmaids, and stacks of flowers and bouquets. Afterwards, there was a lavish wedding feast provided by the Red Cross and comprising many luxuries like coffee and sweets, things not normally obtainable. The couple did not need to go anywhere for their honeymoon. They stayed in this gay and pleasant town, it's name? Belsen.' What did you think about the article when you read it the first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a pack of lies from beginning to end. The camp was closed mid-May 1945. And he said all these things happened in August. It's a whole fabrication of lies. Because he could not possibly have seen all these things and covered the area in two days unless he was some kind of jet-propelled Superman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in the picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who's me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Charles Salt. Taken in Belsen. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Rough date? When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It would be August. August 1945. August 1945. This is where people went in to have a shower and came out the other end clean and get a change of clothing. But at first, they were frightened to go in because they thought it's the same showers as they had in Auschwitz, where they went in and they walked straight into the gas chambers. But eventually they all went in, and they'd come out the other end. So it was all right. They left all their old clothes behind them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the entrance to the concentration camp. And this-- this notice was put up. This one's in English, and then on the other side, there was one in German, put up by the British. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was the gallows in the-- in the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which concentration-- which camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in the Belsen concentration camp. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This was one of the mass graves. But it's not there no more. It's all been landscaped, and there's a little plaque on it. It was 1,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Roughly when was this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, August 1945. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was one of the trenches where the mass graves took place. Where people were buried with a bulldozer, just shoved in with a bulldozer. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was the SS quarters in the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Belsen, yeah. This was the crematorium in Belsen camp. And the soldier in the photo, it was his camera. So he wanted to be in the film. His name was Charlie Carruthers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] This is the graves where people were buried individually. If you knew someone, a friend or relative, so they got an individual grave. And they put on a headboard, a piece of cardboard, a piece of wood, whatever they can find, they put it there. And when I was there last year for the 50th anniversary, this has all disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [INAUDIBLE] I was told that all these people have been re-interred in the tank training barracks, now renamed [? Hohner ?] Barracks. And I asked the official there, I said, how did you get all these graves into the small cemetery that they made? It's a small corner of a field. So he said, to tell you the truth, I didn't want to tell you, but they're buried four deep. They're not individual graves. They are all buried four deep. And so I said, well, what happened to the headboards with their names on them? He said he don't know. So nobody knows where they are.
